---
title: "Day 7: From Numbers to a Movie"
subtitle: "Morning brief, Tuesday July 21"
author: "Dan Negrut"
date: 2026-07-21
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    width: 1250
    margin: 0.08
    transition: fade
    footer: "ProCSI C Training  |  Day 7"
    embed-resources: true
---

## Where this fits

- Yesterday you computed **where the brick is at every instant**; today you make those numbers visible.
- This is exactly how professional simulation movies are made: a solver produces positions over time, and a renderer turns them into frames.
- Everything you need you already have: yesterday's CSV files plus your day-4 Blender skills.

## Today's target

- **By 4:00, your team can:** play a rendered Blender **movie of the brick sliding**, driven by your day-6 CSV, over a real-looking backdrop. A white empty void does not count; give the eye something to measure motion against.
- **Bonus:** a second object in the scene for scale (a tree, a road, a row of cones).

## How to start

- Tools: Blender, plus yesterday's CSV files.
- **Starter:** `starters/day-07-blender/animate_brick.py`, a helper script: select your brick object, run the script in Blender's **Scripting** tab, and it turns each CSV row into a keyframe. A sample CSV sits next to it in case yesterday's file is lost.
- Ask Claude: "What is a keyframe in Blender?" or "How do I render an animation to an MP4 file in Blender?"

## Making it look like a movie {.smaller}

- The script maps simulation time to frames at 24 fps: 10 s of sliding becomes 240 frames.
- Backdrop ideas: a large ground plane with a texture (e.g., a checkerboard), or a sky via **World Properties**; even a single photo as a backdrop plane works.
- Render settings: **Eevee**, 1280x720, output format **FFmpeg Video** (MP4); then **Ctrl+F12** renders the animation.
- Budget your time: 240 frames take a while; do a small test render of 20 frames first.
- The brick travels 250 m; either pull the camera way back, or have the camera follow the brick (parent the camera to it, or keyframe the camera too).

## Make the physics more challenging {.smaller}
- Make the brick slide down on an inclined plane.
    - Play with the friction coefficient $\mu$ to see how it affects the motion.
    - Give the bring an initial velocity up the plane.
    - Give the bring an initial velocity down the plane. See if it can slide but stop before the end of the plane.
- Generate nice movies for the different cases.

## Your end-of-day talk

- ~10 minutes: play your movies on the projector; tell us one rendering trick you discovered.
- **Start or Close with your team's quote of the day.**

\

- Stretch goal: write the equations of motion of two bricks that are on a flat surface and are connected by a spring-damper element. 
    - Choose the parameters of the spring-damper element to make the bricks oscillate. Give the bricks some initial velocity to make them oscillate and then generate a movie of the motion.
